content.text: When a quality gate raises a failure — stale content, incomplete metadata, or truncated chunks — the system must not just log the problem but route it through a structured remediation workflow that tracks the issue to resolution, enforces SLAs, and automatically unblocks retrieval once the fix is verified.
Every quality failure receives a type and severity before routing. The type determines which remediation path applies; the severity determines the SLA.
embedding_created_at too old or model changed
Required metadata field absent from chunk
Partial — if catalog has the value
Fewer chunks than minimum for content class
Origin URL returns error or auth fails
No data classification label on chunks

from __future__ import annotations
import enum
import uuid
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

class IssueType(enum.Enum):
    STALE_SOURCE        = "stale_source"
    INGESTION_LAG       = "ingestion_lag"
    EMBEDDING_DRIFT     = "embedding_drift"
    MISSING_FIELD       = "missing_field"
    CHUNK_COUNT_LOW     = "chunk_count_low"
    COVERAGE_LOW        = "coverage_low"
    SOURCE_UNREACHABLE  = "source_unreachable"
    CLASSIFICATION_MISSING = "classification_missing"

class Severity(enum.Enum):
    CRITICAL = "critical"   # both freshness + completeness fail
    HIGH     = "high"       # regulatory stale or classification missing
    MEDIUM   = "medium"     # standard stale or chunk count low
    LOW      = "low"        # warn-level freshness drift

class RemediationStatus(enum.Enum):
    OPEN        = "open"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    RESOLVED    = "resolved"
    ESCALATED   = "escalated"
    WONT_FIX    = "wont_fix"   # source permanently retired

# SLA in hours by severity
REMEDIATION_SLA_HOURS: dict[Severity, float] = {
    Severity.CRITICAL: 1.0,
    Severity.HIGH:     4.0,
    Severity.MEDIUM:   24.0,
    Severity.LOW:      72.0,
}

@dataclass
class RemediationIssue:
    issue_id: str
    doc_id: str
    issue_type: IssueType
    severity: Severity
    violations: list[str]
    opened_at: datetime
    sla_deadline: datetime
    status: RemediationStatus = RemediationStatus.OPEN
    assigned_to: Optional[str] = None   # job ID or steward email
    resolved_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    resolution_note: Optional[str] = None
    attempts: int = 0

    @classmethod
    def create(
        cls,
        doc_id: str,
        issue_type: IssueType,
        severity: Severity,
        violations: list[str],
    ) -> "RemediationIssue":
        now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
        sla_h = REMEDIATION_SLA_HOURS[severity]
        return cls(
            issue_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
            doc_id=doc_id,
            issue_type=issue_type,
            severity=severity,
            violations=violations,
            opened_at=now,
            sla_deadline=now + timedelta(hours=sla_h),
        )

    def is_sla_breached(self) -> bool:
        return (
            self.status not in {RemediationStatus.RESOLVED, RemediationStatus.WONT_FIX}
            and datetime.now(timezone.utc) > self.sla_deadline
        )

    def mark_resolved(self, note: str = "") -> None:
        self.status = RemediationStatus.RESOLVED
        self.resolved_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
        self.resolution_note = note

The re-ingest path handles staleness, low chunk count, and coverage failures. It fetches the current source content, re-splits it, re-embeds, and upserts all resulting chunks into the vector store — updating every timestamp field.

from __future__ import annotations
import logging
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable, Any

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@dataclass
class ReIngestionResult:
    doc_id: str
    success: bool
    chunks_produced: int
    error: str = ""

def run_re_ingestion(
    issue: RemediationIssue,
    fetch_source: Callable[[str], str],          # doc_id → raw text
    chunk_and_embed: Callable[[str, str], list[dict]],  # (doc_id, text) → chunks
    upsert_chunks: Callable[[list[dict]], None],
    update_catalog: Callable[[str, datetime], None],  # doc_id, ingested_at
    *,
    max_attempts: int = 3,
) -> ReIngestionResult:
    """Fetch the source, re-chunk, re-embed, and upsert."""
    for attempt in range(1, max_attempts + 1):
        issue.attempts = attempt
        try:
            raw_text = fetch_source(issue.doc_id)
            chunks = chunk_and_embed(issue.doc_id, raw_text)
            # Each chunk must carry updated timestamps
            now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
            for c in chunks:
                c.setdefault("metadata", {})["ingested_at"] = now
                c["metadata"]["embedding_created_at"] = now

            upsert_chunks(chunks)
            update_catalog(issue.doc_id, datetime.now(timezone.utc))

            issue.mark_resolved(
                f"re-ingested on attempt {attempt}: {len(chunks)} chunks produced"
            )
            log.info("Re-ingestion OK: %s (%d chunks)", issue.doc_id, len(chunks))
            return ReIngestionResult(
                doc_id=issue.doc_id, success=True, chunks_produced=len(chunks)
            )
        except Exception as exc:
            log.warning("Re-ingestion attempt %d failed for %s: %s", attempt, issue.doc_id, exc)

    issue.status = RemediationStatus.ESCALATED
    msg = f"Re-ingestion failed after {max_attempts} attempts"
    return ReIngestionResult(doc_id=issue.doc_id, success=False, chunks_produced=0, error=msg)
When required metadata fields are missing but the values can be derived from the source catalog (e.g.,
), a field patch job reads the canonical values and writes them to every affected chunk without re-fetching the full source.

Without safeguards, a remediation system can enter a loop: a re-ingest fails to update a timestamp, the scanner detects the same failure, and a new issue is opened before the first is closed. Three rules prevent this.
Before opening a new issue, check whether an open or in-progress issue already exists for the same
pair. If one exists, increment its
counter rather than creating a duplicate.
The verification pass runs after the fix job completes — not as a pre-flight. This ensures the scanner only blocks the document until the fix is confirmed, not for the duration of the fix job.
If a document has failed the same issue type three or more times in 24 hours, escalate to ESCALATED and suppress further auto-remediation until a steward intervenes.

from __future__ import annotations
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from typing import Optional

class RemediationRegistry:
    """Tracks open issues and prevents duplicate or runaway remediation."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._open: dict[tuple[str, str], RemediationIssue] = {}   # (doc_id, type) → issue
        self._history: list[RemediationIssue] = []

    def get_or_create(
        self,
        doc_id: str,
        issue_type: IssueType,
        severity: Severity,
        violations: list[str],
    ) -> tuple[RemediationIssue, bool]:
        """Returns (issue, is_new). is_new=False means an existing open issue was found."""
        key = (doc_id, issue_type.value)
        existing = self._open.get(key)
        if existing and existing.status in {
            RemediationStatus.OPEN, RemediationStatus.IN_PROGRESS
        }:
            existing.violations = violations  # refresh with latest violation details
            return existing, False

        issue = RemediationIssue.create(doc_id, issue_type, severity, violations)
        self._open[key] = issue
        return issue, True

    def close(self, issue: RemediationIssue) -> None:
        key = (issue.doc_id, issue.issue_type.value)
        self._open.pop(key, None)
        self._history.append(issue)

    def recent_failure_count(self, doc_id: str, issue_type: IssueType, window_h: float = 24) -> int:
        cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(hours=window_h)
        return sum(
            1 for i in self._history
            if i.doc_id == doc_id
            and i.issue_type == issue_type
            and i.opened_at > cutoff
            and i.status != RemediationStatus.RESOLVED
        )

    def should_suppress(self, doc_id: str, issue_type: IssueType) -> bool:
        return self.recent_failure_count(doc_id, issue_type) >= 3

After a fix job completes, the system re-runs the original quality checks against the updated document. Only a clean pass clears the block; a continued failure re-classifies the issue and may escalate it.

from __future__ import annotations
from typing import Callable

def verify_and_clear(
    issue: RemediationIssue,
    registry: RemediationRegistry,
    run_quality_check: Callable[[str], "DocumentQualityReport"],
    unblock_document: Callable[[str], None],
    notify_steward_fn: Callable,
) -> None:
    """Re-run quality checks; clear block if passing; escalate if still failing."""
    report = run_quality_check(issue.doc_id)

    if report.status.value == "pass":
        unblock_document(issue.doc_id)
        issue.mark_resolved("post-fix verification passed")
        registry.close(issue)
        return

    # Still failing — escalate if too many attempts
    if registry.should_suppress(issue.doc_id, issue.issue_type):
        issue.status = RemediationStatus.ESCALATED
        notify_steward_fn(
            issue,
            reason=f"3+ failed remediation attempts; manual investigation required. "
                   f"Remaining violations: {report.all_violations}",
        )
        registry.close(issue)
    else:
        # Reset to OPEN for the next remediation cycle
        issue.status = RemediationStatus.OPEN
        issue.violations = report.all_violations
SLA clock starts at issue creation, not fix-job start.
Queueing delay counts. If the remediation job queue is backed up, SLA breaches will occur even if the fix itself is fast — monitor queue depth alongside issue age.
Mark an issue RESOLVED only after the post-fix quality check passes — not when the fix job returns success. Job success and quality-check success are different conditions.
indicate a root-cause problem (broken source connector, wrong content class config). Suppress auto-remediation after three attempts and require a steward to diagnose first.
Every issue, attempt, outcome, and resolution note must be stored. Regulators auditing data governance need to show that quality failures were detected and resolved within SLA.
Treating WONT_FIX as a silent close.
When a source is permanently retired, mark chunks as
in the vector store and remove them from retrieval rather than leaving them blocked-but-present.

A quality remediation workflow closes the loop between detection and resolution: every quality failure is classified by type and severity, routed to an automatic fix job or steward escalation, re-verified after the fix, and only cleared once the knowledge base passes all checks. De-duplication, back-off on repeated failures, and SLA monitoring prevent runaway automation while ensuring issues are resolved within the time frame that the data governance policy requires.
